text: I want to start by putting this in perspective in terms of the loss of human life, you say that climate change will be many times or could be many times more deadly than the COVID-19 pandemic.
Make that connection for us. How much worse could it be.
Yeah, so the deaths will just go up over time as you get more heat waves, forest fires, and most importantly, the inability to go outdoors and do farming.
Anywhere near the equator and so it'll get worse and worse. The temperature rise is based on the total cumulative amount of CO2 we put in the atmosphere.
And so certainly by the end of the system, the century, you'll have permanent diodes of all these coral ecosystems. You know, beach you ever went to will be around.
And sadly there won't be a simple solution like, hey, let's get a vaccine and we can get ourselves out of this thing because it's the entire industrial economy that's responsible for these emissions.

Now you say we have no choice but to get to zero from 51 billion tons a year and I know you're optimistic. You say it'll be hard. But I want to hear from Bill Gates the realist like how likely is it that we get to zero. Is there a percentage you can give me.

Well, unless the younger generation adopts this as the moral cause that's important to them beyond their individual success and they're very loud about that in all the rich countries and across multiple political parties.

Unless we have that that's always putting it first on the agenda that I don't think will meet 2050 but I do see signs that that's coming into place. I mean, you know, what cause could be a more important than preserving livability of the earth and these natural ecosystems.

So my role was simply to say yes, this goal is a good one although very very hard and then talk about how we accelerate the innovation and make people realize that because we're trying to get to zero it's every sector of the mission.

And there's been a tendency to focus on two of the five electrosty and transport but kind of ignore agriculture and buildings and then manufacturing including cement and steel. So it's a it's hopefully a dose of realism for anyone who thought it was easy but also a roadmap to help people who think it's impossible to see that it is possible.

I do think people get bought into these manias who may not have as much money to spare.
So, I'm not bullish on Bitcoin and, you know, my general thought would be that, you know, if you have less money than Elon, you should probably watch out.
Why aren't you bullish on Bitcoin?
It's, look, there are things we invest in in society that produce output.
Bitcoin happens to use a lot of energy. It happens to promote anonymous transactions. They're not reversible transactions.
The Gates Foundation does a lot in terms of digital currency.
But those are things where you can see who's making the transaction.
So, digital money is a good thing.
But, you know, there's a different approach that's local currency and attribute it and deals with all the money laundering and terrorism type regulations.
And yet, gives you the very convenience and the low cost of transaction.
And so, you know, our foundation is very proud that in the pandemic a lot of the countries we funded to do this, we're able to get money out to their citizens very, very efficiently.
So, that's something that it's not got the visibility a Bitcoin, but the move towards digital money that we're very engaged in is a super positive thing.
That eventually will get to even the poorest countries.
So, let's talk about the pandemic.
Obviously, your other big focus in addition to climate change, the US just hit 500,000 deaths.
And I just wanted to take a moment there. How does that number strike you?
Well, it's unbelievably it's, you know, more than any war we've been in, except the civil war, you know, it's enough that I'm sure it's touched almost everybody.
You know, we, we count on government to both before something bad happens and when something bad happens to protect us and it didn't work in this case.
And hopefully it's a lesson going forward about investments in global health that the US and the US cooperating with other countries need to get done to be ready for the next pandemic.
How concerned are you about the new variants and vaccines keeping up.
Well, amazingly, our foundation was studying three vaccines in trials in South Africa when the variant showed up.

We were able to gather really detailed data showing that, you know, they all still had some beneficial effect, but particularly no-bebacks in Johnson-Bron and Johnson even though they worked a little less well.
We're still a great tool there.
And, you know, so weirdly the US didn't sequence enough to see its variants like the UK and even what we funded in South Africa.
Now that's being remedied just another miscued by the country that should have done the best job.
So we'll get on top of the variants, you know, we may need to tune the vaccines, all the manufacturers are looking through how quickly they could get that done.

Our Pfizer, Moderna, Johnson and Johnson doing enough to prioritize populations in developing countries and what's the ideal shot for the developing world.
